name: org.machanism.machai:genai-client
description: GenAI Client is a Java library for provider-agnostic integration with generative AI services in the MachAI ecosystem. It gives JVM applications a consistent API to resolve a provider from a Provider:Model identifier, initialize it from configuration, submit prompts and system instructions, execute model requests, track token usage, expose host-side functional tools, and optionally generate embedding vectors. The library centers on org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai as the main abstraction, org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager as the provider factory, org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.AbstractAIProvider as the shared base implementation, and org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools plus org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader as the SPI for discovering and registering local tools through java.util.ServiceLoader. Included provider implementations are org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.openai.OpenAIProvider for OpenAI-compatible Responses API usage with function tools, web search, MCP server registration, and usage tracking; org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.claude.ClaudeProvider for Anthropic Claude models with tool calling, web search variants, MCP server support, and token accounting; and org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.codemie.CodeMieProvider for EPAM CodeMie authentication and delegation to OpenAI-compatible or Anthropic-compatible backends depending on the configured model family. Configuration is supplied through org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator using keys such as chatModel, OPENAI_API_KEY, OPENAI_BASE_URL, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL, GENAI_TIMEOUT, MAX_OUTPUT_TOKENS, MAX_TOOL_CALLS, WebSearchTool.type, MCP.name, MCP.url, GENAI_USERNAME, GENAI_PASSWORD, and AUTH_URL. Host applications can register custom functional tools through org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai.addTool(java.lang.String, java.lang.String, org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.ToolFunction, java.lang.String...) and can discover reusable tool installers through the FunctionTools SPI. The library is suitable for prompt orchestration, LLM-backed automation, AI-assisted developer workflows, semantic search support via embeddings, and integration of external capabilities such as web search, local command execution, REST calls, and MCP servers into controlled AI workflows.

usageContext: JVM applications integrating multiple AI providers
Provider-agnostic prompt execution and response generation
Host-managed tool calling with local Java functions
Embedding generation for semantic search and retrieval
Configuration-driven MCP and web search integration

constructors.description: Creates and initializes a concrete org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai implementation from a provider spec formatted as Provider:Model or a fully qualified provider class name. The method derives the provider implementation class, stores the resolved model in the Configurator under chatModel, invokes init(org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator), and returns the provider ready for prompting, tool registration, and execution.

examples.description: Creates an OpenAI-backed provider for a typical application workflow. Step 1: create a Configurator. Step 2: set authentication and timeout properties. Step 3: resolve the provider using the Provider:Model identifier. Step 4: use the returned provider for instructions, prompts, tools, and perform().

Creates a CodeMie-backed provider that authenticates with EPAM CodeMie and delegates to a downstream provider based on the model family. This is a practical setup for organizations using CodeMie as the access point for hosted AI services.

constructors.description: Constructs a tool loader that discovers all org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools implementations available through java.util.ServiceLoader. The loader can then apply those installers to a provider instance so that the model can call host-managed functions during execution.

examples.description: Creates the loader before provider setup. In a real-world scenario, instantiate the loader once during application bootstrap, then apply discovered tool installers to each provider instance that should support functional tools.

features.name: prompt
features.description: Adds a user prompt to the current provider session. Providers accumulate prompt messages until perform() is called, allowing the host application to build a structured interaction step by step.

examples.description: How to use it: 1) install the Maven dependency for genai-client, 2) configure OPENAI_API_KEY, 3) create the provider, 4) call instructions(), 5) add a prompt, and 6) execute perform() to receive the generated response.

features.name: instructions
features.description: Sets system-level instructions that shape how the model should respond. This is useful for enforcing output style, role, domain constraints, or formatting requirements separately from the user prompt itself.

examples.description: How to use it: set instructions before prompts when you need stable response behavior across multiple prompts. This is especially useful in enterprise workflows where consistent style and policy guidance are required.

features.name: perform
features.description: Executes the current provider request using accumulated prompts, instructions, configured tools, and provider-specific settings. Compatible providers can iterate through model-requested function calls and continue the exchange until a final text response is produced.

examples.description: How to use it: build the conversation state first, then call perform() once to send the request. In tool-enabled scenarios, perform() can transparently handle one or more model-initiated tool calls before returning the final answer.

features.name: addTool
features.signature: public void org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.openai.OpenAIProvider.addTool(java.lang.String name, java.lang.String description, org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.ToolFunction function, java.lang.String... paramsDesc)
features.description: Registers a host-managed function tool for the OpenAI provider. Parameter descriptor strings are converted into a JSON-schema-like object definition so the model can understand the tool signature and invoke the matching org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.ToolFunction implementation at runtime.

examples.description: How to use it: 1) add the library dependency to Maven, 2) configure the OpenAI API key, 3) create the provider, 4) optionally set a working directory, 5) register the tool with parameter descriptors, 6) prompt the model to use the tool, and 7) run perform().

features.name: applyTools
features.description: Discovers all available org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools implementations on the classpath, creates fresh instances, injects the shared Configurator, and registers their tools on the supplied provider. This allows modular tool bundles to be activated without hard-coding each tool in application code.

examples.description: How to use it: 1) install the dependency in pom.xml, 2) ensure ServiceLoader metadata for tool installers is on the classpath, 3) build the provider, 4) apply discovered tools through the loader, and 5) execute prompts that can benefit from host-managed tools.

features.name: embedding
features.description: Generates an embedding vector for the supplied text through the delegated OpenAI-compatible backend used by CodeMie. This feature supports retrieval and semantic matching use cases where vector representations of text are required.

examples.description: How to use it: 1) configure CodeMie credentials, 2) initialize the provider with an embedding-capable model identifier, 3) call embedding() with the input text and desired dimensions, and 4) use the returned vector in search indexing or similarity comparison workflows.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai
customizations.description: Primary extension point for adding a new provider integration. Implementers supply initialization, prompt handling, tool registration, request execution, usage reporting, input logging, and working-directory propagation behind a stable provider-neutral API.

examples.description: Shows how a project can create its own provider implementation and keep the rest of the application code bound only to the Genai abstraction.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools
customizations.description: SPI for contributing reusable tool bundles that can be discovered through java.util.ServiceLoader and applied to a provider at runtime. This is the preferred extension point for packaging related tools independently from application bootstrap code.

examples.description: Demonstrates how to define a custom tool installer that contributes one callable tool. Register the implementation in META-INF/services/org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools to make it discoverable.

customizations.name: WebSearchTool and MCP configuration
customizations.description: Providers derived from AbstractAIProvider can be customized through Configurator properties that enable built-in web search and MCP server registration. Relevant keys include WebSearchTool.type, WebSearchTool.city, WebSearchTool.country, WebSearchTool.region, MCP.name, MCP.url, MCP.description, MCP.authorization, and numbered variants such as MCP_1.name and MCP_1.url.

examples.description: Shows a practical configuration-driven customization where web search and an MCP server are enabled without changing provider code.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai
studs.description: Core provider contract used by client code to interact with any supported AI backend through a unified set of operations for prompting, configuration, execution, logging, and token usage inspection.

examples.description: Illustrates direct use of the interface so provider selection stays configuration-driven and business code remains independent of the concrete backend.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider
studs.description: Dedicated abstraction for providers that can create vector embeddings from text. It is used when semantic search, ranking, or retrieval workflows need a provider-specific embedding implementation.

examples.description: Shows how an embedding-capable provider can be resolved separately from the main Genai conversation API for vectorization workflows.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools
studs.description: Tool installer SPI intended for implementation by modules that want to publish one or more reusable functional tools to a compatible provider.

examples.description: Demonstrates a minimal SPI implementation that contributes a tool to the provider through the shared registration contract.

examples.title: Install the library as a Maven dependency and execute a basic prompt
steps: Add the Maven dependency for org.machanism.machai:genai-client:1.1.14-SNAPSHOT to your pom.xml.
Make sure your project runs on Java 8 or newer.
Set the OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable or provide the same value through org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator.
Create a Configurator and resolve a provider using org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("OpenAI:gpt-4.1", conf).
Call instructions() and prompt() to prepare the request.
Call perform() to send the request and read the generated answer.
examples.snippet:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.machanism.machai</groupId>
  <artifactId>genai-client</artifactId>
  <version>1.1.14-SNAPSHOT</version>
</dependency>

org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator conf = new org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator();
conf.set("OPENAI_API_KEY", System.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"));
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider = org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("OpenAI:gpt-4.1", conf);
provider.instructions("You are a helpful Java assistant.");
provider.prompt("Explain what a provider abstraction solves in one paragraph.");
java.lang.String answer = provider.perform();
examples.description: Practical how-to scenario: install the library, configure credentials, create a provider, and run a simple text generation request. This is the fastest path to integrating GenAI Client into a JVM application.

examples.title: Configure and use CodeMie as the provider backend
steps: Add the genai-client Maven dependency to your application.
Provide GENAI_USERNAME and GENAI_PASSWORD through environment variables or Configurator values.
Optionally set AUTH_URL if your organization uses a non-default CodeMie authentication endpoint.
Resolve the provider with a CodeMie-prefixed model such as CodeMie:gpt-4o-mini or CodeMie:claude-3-5-sonnet.
Use prompt() and perform() exactly as you would with other providers because the API remains provider-agnostic.
examples.description: Real-world usage scenario for organizations that authenticate against EPAM CodeMie and want to keep application code unchanged while switching backend model families through configuration.

examples.title: Install, configure, and run functional tools through ServiceLoader
steps: Add the genai-client dependency to your Maven build so the provider and tool SPI APIs are available.
Create a FunctionTools implementation in your application or an auxiliary library.
Register that implementation in META-INF/services/org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools so java.util.ServiceLoader can discover it.
Configure the provider with the required API key and create it through GenaiProviderManager.
Create a FunctionToolsLoader and call applyTools(provider, conf) to register discovered tools.
Prompt the model with a task that can benefit from the installed tools, then call perform().
examples.snippet:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.machanism.machai</groupId>
  <artifactId>genai-client</artifactId>
  <version>1.1.14-SNAPSHOT</version>
</dependency>

public final class com.example.tools.TimeFunctionTools implements org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools {
  public void applyTools(org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider) {
    provider.addTool("current_time", "Returns the current UTC timestamp", (com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.JsonNode params, java.io.File workingDir) -> java.time.Instant.now().toString());
  }
}

org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator conf = new org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator();
conf.set("OPENAI_API_KEY", System.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"));
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider = org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("OpenAI:gpt-4.1", conf);
provider.setWorkingDir(new java.io.File("."));
org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader loader = new org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader();
loader.applyTools(provider, conf);
provider.instructions("Use the current_time tool when the user asks for time.");
provider.prompt("What time is it according to the current_time tool?");
java.lang.String answer = provider.perform();
examples.description: Step-by-step how-to for exposing host-managed functions to the model. The example covers installation, registration, configuration, and execution of a real tool-calling workflow.

examples.title: Enable built-in web search and MCP server integration through configuration
steps: Add the Maven dependency for genai-client.
Set OPENAI_API_KEY in the environment or Configurator.
Configure WebSearchTool.type and optional location properties to enable provider-native web search.
Configure MCP.name and MCP.url, plus optional description and authorization, to attach an MCP server.
Create the provider through GenaiProviderManager and execute a prompt that may require external context or remote tools.
examples.description: Shows a configuration-first setup where the application enables provider-native capabilities without custom tool code. This is useful when the application wants web retrieval or remote MCP tools with minimal Java customization.
